question: What does Ch.01 state about the NuScale spent fuel pool design, and what implications does the 24-month refueling cycle have for Ch.05 RCS operations?

nuscale_ch01_0043.title: Ultimate Heat Sink
nuscale_ch01_0043.summary: The Ultimate Heat Sink for the NuScale plant is a large, stainless steel-lined, reinforced concrete pool located below grade, comprising the reactor, refueling, and spent fuel pool areas. During normal operations, heat is removed via the reactor pool cooling system and rejected to the atmosphere, while in the event of a total loss of AC power, decay heat is passively transferred to the pool, which has sufficient water inventory to ensure continued cooling through heat-up and boiling.

nuscale_ch01_0303.title: DSRS 9.1.3, Rev 0: Spent Fuel Pool Cooling and Cleanup SystemII.5 Specific Criteria to Meet GDC 63
nuscale_ch01_0303.summary: This section summarizes the NuScale Spent Fuel Pool Cooling and Cleanup System’s conformance with regulatory criteria, specifically addressing compliance with General Design Criteria (GDC) 63. The system fully meets the requirements of GDC 63, with no exceptions noted, as referenced in sections 9.1.3.1 and 9.1.3.5 of the report.

nuscale_ch01_0301.title: DSRS 9.1.3, Rev 0: Spent Fuel Pool Cooling and Cleanup SystemII.3 Specific Criteria to Meet GDC 5
nuscale_ch01_0301.summary: Section II.3 of DSRS 9.1.3, Rev 0, states that the Spent Fuel Pool Cooling and Cleanup System fully conforms to the specific criteria required to meet General Design Criterion (GDC) 5, with no exceptions noted. This indicates that the system design ensures structures, systems, and components important to safety are not shared among multiple units in a way that could compromise safety functions.

nuscale_ch01_0051.title: Plant Features of Special Interest
nuscale_ch01_0051.summary: This section describes special plant features of the NuScale Power Plant, highlighting structures such as the Central Utility Building and Diesel Generator Buildings, which house essential but non-safety-related equipment and services. It also emphasizes human factors engineering, noting that the plant design minimizes human error through automation, digital controls, and optimized workload management, in compliance with regulatory requirements.

nuscale_ch01_0439.title: SRP 10.4.6, Rev 3: Condensate Cleanup SystemII.2 Maintain indirect cycle PWR water quality to avoid corrosion-induced failure of the reactor coolant pressure boundary (GDC 14)
nuscale_ch01_0439.summary: The section outlines NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for maintaining indirect cycle PWR water quality to prevent corrosion-induced failure of the reactor coolant pressure boundary, as required by GDC 14. It notes that the NuScale steam generator design places primary water outside and secondary water inside the tubes, with no steam generator blowdown, resulting in secondary chemistry requirements that differ from standard guidelines.

nuscale_ch01_0294.title: DSRS 9.1.2, Rev 0: New and Spent Fuel StorageII.3 Specific Criteria to Meet GDC 5
nuscale_ch01_0294.summary: The section demonstrates that the NuScale design fully conforms to the specific criteria of DSRS 9.1.2, Rev 0, Section II.3, which addresses the requirements of GDC 5 for new and spent fuel storage. No exceptions or additional requirements are noted, and relevant compliance details are referenced in sections 9.1.2.1 and 9.1.2.3 of the report.

nuscale_ch01_0790.title: SRP 15.7.4, Rev 1: Radiological Consequences of Fuel Handling AccidentsII.1 Acceptability of Site and Dose Mitigating ESF Systems
nuscale_ch01_0790.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for radiological consequences of fuel handling accidents. It notes that certain acceptance criteria from SRP 15.7.4 are not applicable or are superseded by newer guidance (SRP 15.0.3), while others only partially apply due to differences in NuScale’s reactor design, such as the absence of a traditional containment building. Overall, the NuScale design addresses relevant criteria where applicable and explains deviations based on its unique features.

nuscale_ch01_0054.title: Requirements for Additional Technical Information
nuscale_ch01_0054.summary: This section outlines the verification and confirmation testing programs for unique design features of the NuScale Power Plant, which are essential to support its safety analysis. It details completed and ongoing tests focused on areas lacking prior data or operational experience, including fuel design, steam generator, control rod assemblies, and integrated system phenomena. Specifically, it describes critical heat flux (CHF) testing for the preliminary fuel design, which informed code development and safety analysis for the final fuel design.

nuscale_ch01_0055.title: NuScale Testing Programs
nuscale_ch01_0055.summary: This section outlines the NuScale testing programs designed to verify and confirm unique design features of the NuScale Power Plant that support its safety analysis. It details completed and ongoing tests focused on areas lacking prior data or operational experience, including fuel design, steam generator, control rod assemblies, and integrated system phenomena. Specifically, it describes critical heat flux (CHF) testing for the preliminary fuel design, which informed code development and safety analysis for the final design.

nuscale_ch01_0791.title: SRP 15.7.4, Rev 1: Radiological Consequences of Fuel Handling AccidentsII.2 Radioactivity Control Features of Fuel Storage and Handling Systems
nuscale_ch01_0791.summary: This section explains that the NuScale design partially conforms to SRP 15.7.4, Rev 1, regarding radioactivity control features for fuel storage and handling systems. Specifically, only the criteria relevant to systems inside the Reactor Building apply, since NuScale does not use a traditional containment building for fuel storage and handling; instead, each module has its own compact steel containment vessel, which does not house these systems. Therefore, requirements for containment-based fuel handling systems are not applicable to the NuScale design.

nuscale_ch01_0287.title: SRP BTP 8-8, (Feb 2012): Onsite (Emergency Diesel Generators) and Offsite Power Sources Allowed Outage Time ExtensionsAll Various
nuscale_ch01_0287.summary: The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report states that requirements for allowed outage times for onsite and offsite AC power sources are not applicable due to the plant’s nonreliance on AC power for safety-related functions. The report also details conformance or partial conformance with various regulatory criteria for electrical systems and fuel storage, noting where certain requirements do not apply to passive plant designs.

nuscale_ch01_0298.title: DSRS 9.1.2, Rev 0: New and Spent Fuel StorageII.7 Criticality Monitors and Subcriticality Margin
nuscale_ch01_0298.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for new and spent fuel storage, specifically addressing criticality monitors and subcriticality margin. It details compliance with various General Design Criteria (GDC) and regulatory requirements, noting that the design meets or partially meets the criteria, with no significant exceptions or required emergency safety features for ventilation. The section also clarifies that certain requirements are not applicable due to the passive nature of the plant design.

nuscale_ch01_0387.title: SRP 9.5.1.1, Rev 0: Fire Protection ProgramII.4 Fire Protection for Permanently Shutdown and Decommissioning Reactor Plants
nuscale_ch01_0387.summary: This section explains that the fire protection requirements for permanently shutdown and decommissioning reactor plants (as outlined in SRP 9.5.1.1, Rev 0, II.4) are not applicable to the NuScale design, since these criteria only apply to reactor licensees who have submitted certifications for license termination under 10 CFR 50.82. The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report clarifies that such provisions do not pertain to their current licensing stage.

nuscale_ch01_0309.title: SRP 9.1.4, Rev 4: Light Load Handling System and Refueling Cavity DesignII.3 Specific Criteria to Meet GDC 61
nuscale_ch01_0309.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for the Light Load Handling System and Refueling Cavity Design, specifically addressing compliance with General Design Criterion (GDC) 61. The document confirms that the design meets the required criteria, with no exceptions or additional comments noted.

nuscale_ch01_0799.title: SRP 15.7.5, Rev 2: Spent Fuel Cask Drop AccidentsII.4 ESF Grade Atmosphere Clean-Up System in Spent Fuel Storage Area
nuscale_ch01_0799.summary: The section explains that the RBC system’s single-failure-proof crane design, in accordance with NUREG-0612 guidelines, ensures that no credible single component failure can result in the loss of load control. Because of this robust design, load drop evaluations and accident analyses for radiological consequences of spent fuel cask or NPM drops are not required, and thus, no such analyses have been performed.

nuscale_ch01_0130.title: 1.9.5 Advanced and Evolutionary Light-Water Reactor Design Issues
nuscale_ch01_0130.summary: This section explains that the NuScale design incorporates operational experience and regulatory guidance, including NRC Bulletins, Generic Letters, and 10 CFR 21 notifications, to ensure safety and compliance. It also addresses advanced and evolutionary light-water reactor design issues, referencing NRC policy and technical guidance such as SECY-93-087 and related staff requirements.

nuscale_ch01_0036.title: Refueling Operations
nuscale_ch01_0036.summary: Refueling operations for a NuScale Power Module (NPM) are conducted independently from other modules. The process involves moving the NPM from its operating bay to the refueling pool, disassembling it for inspection, maintenance, and refueling, and then reassembling and returning it to service after successful testing and startup procedures.

nuscale_ch01_0438.title: SRP 10.4.6, Rev 3: Condensate Cleanup SystemII.1 Maintain direct cycle BWR plant water quality to avoid corrosion-induced failure of the reactor coolant pressure boundary (GDC 14)
nuscale_ch01_0438.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with various regulatory criteria for systems related to condensate cleanup and associated components. It notes that certain requirements are not applicable to NuScale’s design (such as those specific to BWRs), while for others, such as maintaining indirect cycle PWR water quality, NuScale’s design conforms but with differences in secondary chemistry requirements due to its unique steam generator configuration.

edges.description: Section A describes the ultimate heat sink system's design and function for decay heat removal in the reactor pool, while Section B references the ultimate heat sink as the seismic Category I supply system for spent fuel pool cooling, connecting them through the UHS's role in both reactor and spent fuel cooling safety functions.

Section A describes the ultimate heat sink system's design and capabilities, which Section B references as the seismic Category I supply for spent fuel pool cooling under accident conditions, connecting them through the UHS's role in meeting regulatory criteria for spent fuel cooling.

Section A describes the decay heat removal system's reliance on the reactor pool as an ultimate heat sink, while Section B lists the Central Utility Building's nonsafety-related utility functions, indicating that the systems in Section A depend on safety-related structures not covered by the utilities in Section B.

Section A describes the decay heat removal system's design and operation for cooling the reactor coolant system (RCS), while Section B lists regulatory requirements for water quality and material toughness that the NuScale design claims to conform with or not be applicable, connecting them through the need for effective heat removal and water quality maintenance in RCS operations.

Section A and Section B both discuss the NuScale spent fuel pool cooling and cleanup system's conformance with regulatory criteria, specifically referencing the same design features and exceptions regarding seismic categorization and quality group requirements, thus providing parallel evaluations under different General Design Criteria (GDC 63 in A and GDC 5 in B).

Section A discusses the spent fuel pool cooling system's regulatory conformance, specifically noting that the normal makeup water supply is not seismic Category I, while Section B describes the Central Utility Building as housing common utility services, including demineralized water equipment, which is relevant to the spent fuel pool's water supply.

Section A describes the spent fuel pool cooling system's conformance with seismic and quality requirements, while Section B outlines the condensate cleanup system's role in maintaining water quality to prevent reactor coolant pressure boundary corrosion; both sections are connected through their focus on systems that ensure safe reactor operation and compliance with regulatory criteria, particularly regarding water quality and safety functions.

Section A discusses the safety classification and regulatory conformance of the spent fuel pool cooling system, noting that normal makeup water is not seismic Category I, while Section B describes the Central Utility Building as a nonsafety-related structure housing utility services, including demineralized water, which could be relevant to the spent fuel pool's water supply.

Section A discusses the spent fuel pool cooling system's conformance with seismic and quality requirements, while Section B addresses the condensate cleanup system's role in maintaining water quality to prevent corrosion in the reactor coolant system; both sections are connected through their implications for maintaining safe reactor coolant system (RCS) operations, especially over the extended 24-month refueling cycle, as water quality and cooling system integrity are interdependent for long-term operational safety.

Section A describes the physical plant features and utility systems housed in the Central Utility Building, while Section B outlines regulatory requirements for maintaining water quality in the reactor coolant system to prevent corrosion, connecting them through the need for plant infrastructure (from A) to support compliance with water quality standards (from B).

Section A discusses regulatory criteria for spent fuel storage and the nonreliance on AC power for safety functions, while Section B describes the NuScale plant's passive decay heat removal system, both of which are connected by the design's reliance on passive safety features to ensure spent fuel and RCS cooling during extended refueling cycles.

Section A outlines the regulatory context and criteria for spent fuel storage, while Section B details how the NuScale spent fuel pool cooling system design conforms (or partially conforms) to those criteria, specifically referencing seismic and quality group requirements that impact operational implications such as the 24-month refueling cycle.

Section A and Section B both address how the NuScale spent fuel pool design meets regulatory criteria (specifically GDC 5), with Section A focusing on storage and power independence, and Section B detailing the cooling and cleanup system's conformance and exceptions, thus Section B provides specific technical details that elaborate on the general compliance described in Section A.

Section A references regulatory criteria and conformance related to fuel handling accidents and radiological consequences, while Section B describes the NuScale spent fuel pool's ultimate heat sink and decay heat removal systems, with both sections connected through the requirement that the spent fuel pool and RCS operations must ensure adequate cooling and radiological safety during and after refueling cycles.

Section A references the acceptability of site and dose mitigating systems for fuel handling accidents, while Section B specifies the spent fuel pool cooling system's conformance to regulatory criteria, connecting them through the requirement that spent fuel pool design and operations (including refueling cycles) must ensure radiological safety during fuel handling and storage.

Section A discusses regulatory conformance for radiological consequences of fuel handling accidents, while Section B details the spent fuel pool cooling system's design conformance, both connecting through the requirement that the spent fuel pool design (Section B) must support safe fuel handling operations and accident mitigation as evaluated in Section A.

Section A outlines the testing and verification programs for unique NuScale design features that support safety analysis, while Section B describes the ultimate heat sink and decay heat removal systems that are among the features likely subject to such testing, connecting them through the need to validate the performance of critical safety systems.

Section A outlines the testing programs for unique NuScale design features that support safety analysis, which provides evidence for Section B's discussion of how the spent fuel pool cooling system conforms (or partially conforms) to regulatory criteria such as GDC 63.

Section A outlines the testing programs for unique NuScale design features that support safety analysis, while Section B details how the spent fuel pool cooling system design conforms (or partially conforms) to regulatory criteria, with both sections connected through the verification of design features that ensure regulatory compliance.

Section A outlines the testing programs that verify unique NuScale design features supporting safety analysis, while Section B describes the ultimate heat sink system, which is one of the key safety-related design features likely subject to such verification, thus Section A's testing programs provide evidence supporting the safety claims made in Section B about decay heat removal.

Section A describes the testing programs that generate data to support the safety analysis of unique NuScale design features, which are necessary to demonstrate conformance with the regulatory criteria for the spent fuel pool cooling system as outlined in Section B.

Section A describes the testing programs that generate data to support the safety analysis of unique NuScale design features, which are necessary to demonstrate conformance with the regulatory criteria for the spent fuel pool cooling system discussed in Section B.

Section A discusses regulatory conformance and radiological control features related to fuel handling and storage, while Section B describes the NuScale spent fuel pool's ultimate heat sink and decay heat removal systems, with both sections connected through their focus on ensuring safe cooling and radiological safety during fuel handling and storage operations.

Section A discusses regulatory conformance for radiological consequences of fuel handling accidents, while Section B details the spent fuel pool cooling system's compliance with safety criteria, both addressing how the NuScale spent fuel pool design meets regulatory requirements for safety and accident mitigation.

Section A discusses regulatory conformance for radiological consequences of fuel handling accidents in the NuScale spent fuel pool, while Section B details the spent fuel pool cooling system's design conformance with safety criteria, both addressing how the spent fuel pool's design and operational features meet regulatory requirements for safety and accident mitigation.

Section A discusses the inapplicability of AC power outage time restrictions due to NuScale's nonreliance on AC power for safety functions, while Section B describes the passive, closed-loop decay heat removal system that enables this nonreliance by providing cooling without AC power.

Section A outlines the regulatory conformance and operational considerations for spent fuel storage, while Section B specifies the design criteria and exceptions for the spent fuel pool cooling system, with both sections connected by their focus on ensuring the safety and regulatory compliance of the NuScale spent fuel pool design.

Section A outlines the regulatory criteria and conformance for new and spent fuel storage, including criticality and power supply considerations, while Section B details the specific design criteria and conformance for the spent fuel pool cooling system, with both sections connected through their focus on the safety and regulatory compliance of the NuScale spent fuel pool design.

Section A explains that the NuScale design does not rely on engineered safety feature (ESF) ventilation systems for post-accident mitigation, while Section B describes the passive decay heat removal capabilities of the ultimate heat sink, both addressing different aspects of post-accident safety systems and their roles in maintaining safe conditions in the reactor and spent fuel pool.

Section A states that the NuScale design does not rely on engineered safety feature (ESF) ventilation systems for accident mitigation, while Section B specifies that the spent fuel pool cooling system relies on the ultimate heat sink (UHS) for accident conditions, connecting both sections through their discussion of safety system dependencies and design choices for accident scenarios.

Section A outlines regulatory criteria and conformance for the NuScale spent fuel pool and refueling systems, while Section B describes the ultimate heat sink and decay heat removal systems that support safe RCS operations during refueling cycles, connecting them through the operational and safety requirements imposed by the 24-month refueling interval.

Section A describes the operational process of moving a NuScale Power Module (NPM) to the refueling pool during refueling, while Section B outlines the regulatory criteria and conformance status for the spent fuel pool cooling system, connecting them through the need for adequate spent fuel pool cooling and shielding during and after refueling operations.
